attributes.stem:
A 63-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a 12-hour history of increasing pain in the back of her right eye. She reports that her vision from that eye has become blurred. She has had several preceding episodes of right ocular discomfort at night associated with noticing haloes around lights. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 150/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.0°C. Her right eye is red and inflamed (Image). The right pupil is mid-dilated and non-responsive to light. Her intraocular pressure in the right eye is 52 mmHg (12-22) and 20 mmHg in the left. The left eye appears normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This is a case of acute angle closure glaucoma and is an ophthalmological emergency. The increased intra-orbital pressure has been brought on by the obstruction of outflow of aqueous humour. The haloes were due to corneal oedema caused by the transient periods of raised intraocular pressure. This condition can be precipitated by dim light or anticholinergic or sympathomimetic medications. Women are two - four times as likely to develope acute angle closure glaucoma compared to men.

Immediate treatment is required with intravenous acetazolamide, a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that works systemically to decrease the production of aqueous humor, thereby reducing intraocular pressure. Intravenous administration allows for rapid onset of action, making it effective in quickly lowering IOP in acute situations. Patients should be screened for allergies to sulphonomaides due to cross-sensitivity though it is not an absolute contraindication. Patients should also be given topical pilocarpine, brimonidine and timolol and prednisolone acetate 1% to the affected eye and analgesia for the pain.

Intravenous mannitol is an osmotic diuretic that can be used to lower intraocular pressure in acute angle closure glaucoma. While it can be effective, it is generally considered a second-line treatment after acetazolamide. Mannitol works by creating an osmotic gradient that draws fluid out of the eye, but it can cause significant fluid shifts and is contraindicated in patients with heart or renal failure, diabetes or dehydration.

Topical latanoprost is a prostaglandin analogue used in the treatment of open-angle glaucoma to increase aqueous outflow. However, it is not appropriate for acute angle closure glaucoma as it has a slow onset of action (takes hours to days to reach maximum effect) and does not address the underlying mechanism of angle closure.

Definitive treatment involves laser iridotomy to reduce the pupil block.

Chloramphenicol drops and intravenous hydrocortisone would be of no benefit in treating acute angle closure glaucoma. Chloramphenicol is an antibiotic used for bacterial eye infections, while hydrocortisone is a corticosteroid that would not address the immediate need to lower intraocular pressure.
learningPoints: Acute angle closure glaucoma requires prompt diagnosis and rapid decompression.
Intravenous acetazolamide is the first-line treatment for acute angle closure glaucoma to rapidly reduce intraocular pressure.
references: Cronau H, Kankanala RR, Mauger T. Diagnosis and Management of Red Eye in Primary Care. Am Fam Physician. 2010; 81:137-44.
Khondkaryan A, Francis B. Angle-closure glaucoma. American Academy of opthamology. 2013.  Available at: https://www.aao.org/education/munnerlyn-laser-surgery-center/angleclosure-glaucoma-19
